title: WIP: Added Pod Restart policy
body:
Pod restart policies are defined in Kubernetes as operations that happen after a container exits. Lay groundwork for adding restart policies, and allow for containers to inherit restart policies from their pod if they are in one. Current restart policies are "never" (cleanup container after exit) and "always" (start the container after it exits).

Still TODO:
	onFailure - restart a container only when it doesn't exit with 0
	exponential backoff - allow for both always and onFailure to slow down on restarts if they continually exit. This is done in k8s

The TODO require some changes to conmon, so I'm putting this out there for folks to look at and begin to argue about while I look into that :)

I don't think this works as written - we need `container cleanup` to run after every container exits, no exceptions. We'd need Conmon patched to potentially accept multiple exit commands?

(Alternatively, `cleanup` would need to trigger further postrun actions. I don't know if I like that, but it could definitely be worked into a good way of doing this)

Anyways, what exactly are we trying to do here - restart the entire pod on a container in it exiting, or restart just the one container?

@mheon K8s restarts the one container.

on this branch running
podman pod create --restart-policy always --name demo
podman run --pod demo alpine /bin/sh -c 'sleep 10'

has the container restart every 10 seconds, according to podman ps

Though, I do like the idea of adding this to container create. I thought about adding a cli option as well. But that route would avoid conmon changes which I like, though it may get Hacky.

Hm.

I'd honestly be OK making this a container flag as well - Docker has it on normal containers as well, and if we work out the mechanics it ought to work for every container, pod or no.
We can keep the pod handling (if the container doesn't specify, we use the pod value as default), but allow it to be specified per-container as well. Or maybe we allow --restart-policy on containers, but make it conflict with --pod so you have to use the pod handling if you're in a pod?

We'd probably want to make cleanup check restart policy and perform the appropriate action if and only if it detects a state change from running to stopped/exited (save the state before doing the syncContainer(), and see if it's changed afterwards). There might be other places it runs.
SGTM, I'll look into mimicing docker --restart

Further realization - we might not have the appropriate state loaded...

You basically would have to do an UpdateContainer() with the state first to grab the right state from the DB, then save the state, then syncContainer()...

It's gross (we hit the DB twice instead of once), but it should work.

Well this PR is about pod's not containers, although container restart might be a nice by product.

I see there being three states of a container running within a pod when the container exits.

1: Nothing (Default), which is what we have now
2: Container Restarts
3: Pod gets stopped.

Now we might want to differentiate between a container that exits with status == 0, versus a container that exits !=0.  We also want to clarify on restart count.
:umbrella: The latest upstream changes (presumably #2422) made this pull request unmergeable. Please resolve the merge conflicts.
